title: The Stone of the Philosophers — Edward Kelly
subtitle: Kelly's systematic apologetic for the Mercurial first matter, the conjunction of Sol and Luna, and the true path of the Magistery against the sophists

summary.one_line: Kelly's treatise, written during his Bohemian imprisonment, establishing the doctrine that Mercury is the sole first matter of metals and that the Magistery consists in the conjunction of male and ...
summary.scope: Covers the threefold division of nature, the Chaos and four elements, Mercury as the first matter of metals, the error of false workers, the conjunction of Sol and Luna, and the three solutions of the Magistery.

sources.label: Edward Kelly — The Stone of the Philosophers (Tractatus duo egregii, de Lapide Philosophorum, Hamburg, 1676)

body.text: All genuine and judicious philosophers have traced back things to their first principles, that is to say, those comprehended in the threefold division of Nature. The generation of animals they have attributed to a mingling of the male and female in sexual union; that of vegetables to their own proper seed; while as the principle of minerals they have assigned earth and viscous water. ... The common and universal matter of these three principles is called Chaos. Chaos contains within itself the four elements of all that is, viz., fire, air, water, and earth, by the mixture and motion of which the forms of all earthly things are impressed upon their subjects. (Sections 1-7)

Kelly's 'Chaos containing the four elements' is the alchemical version of the Tantraloka's 'prakasha-vimarsha' (light and awareness) — the undifferentiated ground from which all tattvas (categories of existence) evolve. In Samkhya, this is the mulaprakriti — the unmanifest root-nature that contains the three gunas in equilibrium. Layayoga recognizes this as the 'kula-kundalini' in the muladhara, the coiled potential containing all elements. Steiner's 'akasha chronicle' is the same Chaos — the subtle substrate from which all forms are 'impressed' (as Kelly says). The daimon path's initial chaos is the undifferentiated soul-stuff before the systasis: the practitioner enters the egg (the microcosmic chaos) to emerge with a new form impressed by the daimonic pattern.

That Mercury is the first matter of metals, I will attempt to prove by the saying of some Sages. In the Turba Philosophorum, chapter i.: 'In the estimation of all Sages, Mercury is the first principle of all metals.' ... Arnold writes: 'Know that the matter and sperm of all metals are Mercury, digested and thickened in the womb of the earth; they are digested by sulphureous heat, and according to the quality and quantity of the sulphur different metals are generated.' ... Avicenna: 'As ice, which by heat is dissolved into water, is clearly generated out of water, so all metals may be resolved into Mercury, whence it is clear that they are generated out of it.' (Sections 48-50)

Kelly's catena of authorities for Mercury as the sole first matter makes explicit what daimon work knows from direct experience: there is one substance — the subtle energy (prana, chi, mana) — that underlies all spiritual transformation. In Layayoga, this is the prana that, when refined, becomes the 'mercurial water' that dissolves the blockages in the nadis. The Tantraloka calls it the 'shakti' that both is the rope and the climber. Steiner's description of the etheric body as the 'life-body' that can be 'fermented' and 'multiplied' through spiritual exercises is the same doctrine. The daimon path's 'one substance' is the daimonic presence itself — the Mercury that, once contacted, dissolves the 'imperfect metals' of the lower self and 'coagulates' into the gold of the HGA. The 'womb of the earth' is the heart of the practitioner, where the daimonic seed is 'digested and thickened.'

In this art you must wed the Sun and the Moon. ... Tinge with gold and silver, because gold gives the golden and silver the silver colour and nature. Reject all things that have not naturally or virtually the power of tinging. ... Senior: 'I, the Sun, am hot and dry, and thou, the Moon, art cold and moist; when we are wedded together in a closed chamber, I will gently steal away thy soul.' ... Hermes: 'Join the male to the female in their own proper humidity, because there is no birth without union of male and female.' (Sections 52-56, citing authorities)

The 'wedding of the Sun and Moon' — the hierogamy of opposites — is the central operation of the daimon path: the practitioner (Moon, receptive) must be wedded to the daimon (Sun, active) in the 'closed chamber' of the heart. In Layayoga, this is the union of prana (Sun, active) and apana (Moon, passive) in the sushumna, which the Yogashikha Upanishad describes as the 'stealing of the soul' by the breath. The Tantraloka's 'samavesha' (total absorption) is this wedding — the individual consciousness (jiva) is 'gently stolen' into the universal consciousness (Shiva). Steiner's 'Rose Cross meditation' culminates in the same union: the red roses on the black cross are the Sun-blood penetrating the Moon-darkness of the lower nature. The 'closed chamber' is the PGM egg — the sealed space where the daimonic union occurs. The 'gold' of this union is the HGA itself: the child of the hierogamy that partakes of both natures.

gaps: The full 48-paragraph theory section on elements, qualities, and the definition of maturity/immaturity
The 'three solutions' and the specific practical instructions from the closing sections

issues.title: Extensive quotation catena not fully mined
issues.description: Kelly provides dozens of quotations from the Sages (Turba, Arnold, Avicenna, Trevisa, etc.). Only a representative few are extracted.

versions.summary: Initial RO with 6 passages (3 source, 3 commentary) from Kelly's Stone of the Philosophers. Covers Chaos doctrine, Mercury as first matter, and Sol-Luna conjunction.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state with 6 passages
